text: The surgeon dissects the bladder away from the colon, revealing defects in both structures. The bladder defect is closed in two layers, with visible suturing of the tissue.

The surgeon closes the inner layer of the defect with an absorbable suture in a running fashion, noting the bladder's inflammation and thickening. The outer layer is then closed with a longer-lasting absorbable suture, imbricating the adventitial layer and pelvic peritoneum.

After completing the bladder repair, the surgeon proceeds with a robotic sigmoid colectomy. The left ureter is identified in the retroperitoneal space, with visible vermiculation, ensuring its protection before safely mobilizing the colon.

The surgeon identifies the coalescence of the colonic tinea at the rectosigmoid junction. A tunnel is created in the mesocolon, followed by the transection of the distal colon. The proximal colon is then transected at a point ensuring sufficient length for a tension-free anastomosis with the distal colon.

The surgeon uses the fluorescence lens of the da Vinci robot and an injectable green dye to visualize the blood supply at the free edges of the colon. The proximal edge of the stapeline is then removed, revealing the underlying tissue.

The surgeon places the anvil into the colonic lumen and creates a purse-string suture to prepare for the end-to-end anastomosis. The surgical assistant inserts the EEA stapler through the rectum, releasing the spike through the distal staple line. The anvil is then connected to the EEA stapler, completing the anastomosis.

The surgeon performs a leak test by occluding the proximal colon and filling the pelvis with saline. Air is insufflated through the rectum, and the absence of air bubbles confirms an airtight seal at the anastomosis site.
The surgeon removes all sutures and the colonic specimen, marking the completion of the operation.
